VUR results from abnormal ureteral insertion into the bladder trigone (short submucosal tunnel), not ureteric bud branching failure per se.

choices.text: Renal agenesis or multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK)
choices.feedback: Correct! The ureteric bud induces metanephric mesenchyme to differentiate into nephrons (reciprocal induction). Failed ureteric bud development → no induction signal → renal agenesis (unilateral: often asymptomatic; bilateral: Potter sequence — fatal). Abnormal ureteric bud branching/interaction → multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK — non-functioning, cystic, no renal pelvis). Duplicated ureteric bud → duplex collecting system ± ectopic ureter.

stem: Pulmonary surfactant production begins at what gestational age, and what is its clinical significance?

choices.text: Type II pneumocytes begin producing surfactant around weeks 24
choices.feedback: Correct! Type II alveolar cells produce surfactant (primarily dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine/DPPC + surfactant proteins SP-A, B, C, D). Production: begins ~24-28 weeks, matures ~35 weeks. Insufficiency → respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) — alveolar collapse, hyaline membranes, V/Q mismatch. Treatment: exogenous surfactant (rescue therapy), antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone) to accelerate surfactant production if preterm delivery anticipated. L/S ratio ≥ 2:1 or positive PG (phosphatidylglycerol) in amniotic fluid indicates lung maturity.

choices.text: Week 20 — premature infants never have surfactant
choices.feedback: Surfactant production begins around weeks 24-28, with mature levels reached by 35 weeks.
Surfactant production begins well before term. Near-term infants (35+ weeks) typically have adequate surfactant. RDS is primarily a problem of prematurity before 34 weeks.

choices.text: Week 12 — surfactant is present from early development
choices.feedback: The respiratory system is still in the pseudoglandular stage at week 12 (branching airways, no gas exchange structures). Surfactant production requires Type II pneumocyte maturation much later.

explanation: Key detail: 28; mature by 35 weeks — deficiency causes RDS of prematurity.

stem: What congenital anomaly results from a defect in the pleuroperitoneal membrane?

choices.feedback: Correct! The diaphragm forms from four structures: septum transversum (central tendon), pleuroperitoneal membranes (posterolateral), esophageal mesentery (crura), and body wall mesoderm. Bochdalek hernia: posterolateral defect from failed pleuroperitoneal membrane closure → abdominal organs herniate into thorax → lung compression → pulmonary hypoplasia. Left-sided 80-85% (right side closes earlier + liver protection). Presentation: respiratory distress, scaphoid abdomen, bowel sounds in chest. Emergency: intubation (NO bag-mask — distends stomach), surgical repair after stabilization.
Pectus excavatum is a chest wall deformity from excessive costal cartilage growth, not a diaphragmatic defect.
TEF results from tracheoesophageal septum failure, not pleuroperitoneal membrane defects.
Pulmonary sequestration is non-functioning lung tissue with a systemic arterial supply, not related to pleuroperitoneal membrane.

explanation: Correct: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) — most commonly left-sided (Bochdalek).

stem: What are the stages of lung development in order?

choices.feedback: Correct! Lung development stages: Embryonic (weeks 3-7): lung bud from foregut, primary bronchi. Pseudoglandular (weeks 5-17): all conducting airways branch (16 generations — 'gland-like' appearance, no gas exchange). Canalicular (weeks 16-25): respiratory bronchioles, Type I & II pneumocytes begin differentiating, capillaries approximate airways. Saccular (weeks 24-36): terminal saccules (primitive alveoli), surfactant production increases. Alveolar (week 36 to age 8): true alveoli mature, septation increases surface area. Viability threshold (~24 weeks) coincides with canalicular stage when gas exchange first possible.
This is the reverse order. Development proceeds from embryonic to alveolar.
Pseudoglandular comes before canalicular, and saccular before alveolar.
Embryonic stage comes first, and canalicular precedes saccular. The correct order begins with embryonic.

stem: A child has a lateral neck mass anterior to the SCM at the junction of its middle and lower thirds. On CT, the tract passes between the internal and external carotid arteries to the tonsillar fossa. Which branchial cleft anomaly is this?

choices.feedback: Correct! 2nd branchial cleft anomalies are the most common (95%). The tract courses: external opening at anterior border of SCM (junction of middle and lower thirds) → between internal and external carotid arteries → to the tonsillar fossa (2nd pharyngeal pouch). Bailey classification: Type I (superficial to SCM), Type II (most common — deep to SCM, lateral to great vessels), Type III (between ICA and ECA), Type IV (medial to great vessels, opens into pharynx).
4th branchial cleft anomalies are extremely rare, course inferiorly around the aortic arch (left) or subclavian (right), and open into the piriform sinus apex.
1st branchial cleft anomalies present near the ear (pre/postauricular) or angle of the mandible, associated with the external auditory canal.
3rd branchial cleft anomalies are rare and tract passes POSTERIOR to the internal carotid, into the piriform sinus.

stem: What is the standard treatment for a branchial cleft cyst?

choices.text: Complete surgical excision of the cyst and its tract
choices.feedback: Correct! Definitive treatment: complete excision of the cyst AND the entire associated tract (to the pharyngeal pouch origin). Incomplete excision → recurrence. For 2nd branchial cleft: excise tract up to the tonsillar fossa, identifying its course between the carotid arteries. Surgery ideally performed electively when not acutely infected. If infected: antibiotics first ± incision and drainage → delayed definitive excision. Sclerotherapy (e.g., OK-432/picibanil) is an alternative for macrocystic lesions in some centers.
Antibiotics treat secondary infection but do not resolve the cyst. Recurrence is inevitable without excision.
Aspiration provides temporary relief but the cyst recurs. Definitive excision is needed.
Branchial cleft cysts can become repeatedly infected and should be excised. Delaying surgery increases difficulty due to scarring from infections.

stem: Failure of fusion of which embryologic structures causes cleft lip versus cleft palate?

choices.text: Both result from failure of the same fusion event at different time points
choices.feedback: They involve DIFFERENT fusion events and different structures, occurring at different developmental time periods.

choices.text: Cleft lip: failure of fusion of the medial nasal and maxillary prominences
choices.feedback: Correct! Cleft lip: medial nasal prominence (becomes philtrum/premaxilla) fails to fuse with the maxillary prominence → unilateral or bilateral cleft lip ± alveolus (weeks 5-7). Cleft palate: the two palatine shelves fail to elevate and fuse at the midline (weeks 8-12) → cleft of hard and/or soft palate. They are embryologically distinct — can occur independently or together. Cleft lip ± palate: more common in males, multifactorial inheritance. Isolated cleft palate: more common in females, associated with Pierre Robin sequence and other syndromes.

choices.text: Both result from failure of neural crest cell migration to the face
choices.feedback: While neural crest cells contribute to facial development, the specific fusion failures differ between cleft lip and cleft palate.

choices.text: Cleft lip is a neural tube defect; cleft palate is a branchial arch defect
choices.feedback: Neither is a neural tube defect. Both involve facial prominences and branchial arch derivatives.

explanation: Correct: Cleft lip: failure of fusion of the medial nasal and maxillary prominences (weeks 5-7); Cleft palate: failure of fusion of the palatal shelves (weeks 8-12).

stem: What feeding complication is most significant in infants with cleft palate?

choices.feedback: Cleft palate involves the roof of the mouth, not the trachea.

choices.text: Inability to generate adequate negative pressure for sucking → feeding difficulty
choices.feedback: Correct! Intact palate is necessary to create negative intraoral pressure for effective sucking. Cleft palate → air leaks through the nasal cavity → inadequate suction → poor feeding, prolonged feeding times, nasal regurgitation, failure to thrive. Management: specialized nipples (Haberman feeder, Dr. Brown's specialty valve), upright feeding position, frequent burping. Surgical repair (palatoplasty) typically at 9-12 months (before speech development). Also: Eustachian tube dysfunction → recurrent otitis media → hearing loss → PE tube placement.
Cleft palate has no relationship to lactose metabolism. The issue is mechanical — inability to generate suction.

choices.text: Complete inability to swallow requiring G-tube
choices.feedback: Most infants with cleft palate can feed with specialized nipples and positioning. G-tubes are rarely needed.

explanation: Best answer: Inability to generate adequate negative pressure for sucking → feeding difficulty and failure to thrive.

stem: Hemifacial microsomia results from deficiency of which pharyngeal arch?

choices.feedback: 3rd/4th arch deficiency relates to thymus, parathyroid, and great vessel anomalies (DiGeorge spectrum), not facial structures.

choices.text: Neural crest cells from the midbrain only
choices.feedback: While neural crest cells from multiple brain levels contribute, the clinical spectrum is best understood as 1st/2nd pharyngeal arch deficiency.

Correct! Hemifacial microsomia (oculo-auriculo-vertebral spectrum / Goldenhar syndrome): deficient development of 1st and 2nd pharyngeal arches → unilateral mandibular hypoplasia, microtia/anotia (external ear malformation), preauricular tags, macrostomia (lateral oral cleft), facial nerve palsy. 2nd most common facial anomaly after cleft lip/palate. OAVS/Goldenhar adds: epibulbar dermoids, vertebral anomalies. Associated: cardiac defects (30%), renal anomalies. Management: multidisciplinary — mandibular distraction osteogenesis, ear reconstruction, orthodontics.
Lateral plate mesoderm does not form craniofacial structures. The pharyngeal arches (neural crest-derived) are the key structures.

stem: What is the most common external ear anomaly associated with hemifacial microsomia?

choices.feedback: Cauliflower ear results from trauma-induced auricular hematoma, not congenital anomaly.
Preauricular pits/tags are common but microtia is the most significant external ear anomaly in hemifacial microsomia.

choices.text: Microtia — underdeveloped external ear ranging from small but recognizable to complete absence (anotia)
choices.feedback: Correct! Microtia in hemifacial microsomia ranges from Grade I (small but structurally recognizable ear) to Grade IV (anotia — complete absence). Occurs in ~60-70% of hemifacial microsomia patients, usually unilateral. May be accompanied by external auditory canal stenosis/atresia → conductive hearing loss. Management: hearing assessment at birth (BAER testing), bone-anchored hearing aids if needed, surgical ear reconstruction (autologous rib cartilage at age 6-10 or Medpor framework) or prosthetic ear.
Aural atresia can occur but the broader spectrum of external ear malformation is more characteristic.

stem: Which pharyngeal arch gives rise to the muscles of mastication and the mandible?

choices.feedback: 3rd arch: hyoid (greater cornu and lower body), stylopharyngeus muscle. Nerve: CN IX (glossopharyngeal).
4th arch: thyroid cartilage, pharyngeal constrictors, cricothyroid, levator veli palatini. Nerve: CN X (superior laryngeal).

Correct! 1st pharyngeal arch: Meckel's cartilage → mandible, malleus, incus. Muscles: muscles of mastication (temporalis, masseter, lateral/medial pterygoids), mylohyoid, anterior belly of digastric, tensor tympani, tensor veli palatini. Nerve: CN V (trigeminal — mandibular division V3). Mnemonic: '1st arch = Mastication = Mandible = V3.' Deficient 1st arch development → Treacher Collins, Pierre Robin, hemifacial microsomia.
The 2nd arch (Reichert's cartilage) gives rise to the stapes, styloid process, stylohyoid ligament, lesser cornu of hyoid, and muscles of facial expression (CN VII).

explanation: Correct: 1st pharyngeal arch (Meckel's cartilage).

stem: Which pharyngeal arch derivative is innervated by the facial nerve (CN VII)?

choices.feedback: Stylopharyngeus is a 3rd arch muscle innervated by CN IX (glossopharyngeal).

Correct! 2nd pharyngeal arch (Reichert's cartilage): Cartilage → stapes, styloid process, lesser horn of hyoid, stylohyoid ligament, upper body of hyoid. Muscles: muscles of facial expression, stapedius, stylohyoid, posterior belly of digastric. Nerve: CN VII (facial). Mnemonic: '2nd arch = Facial expression = Stapes = CN VII.' Clinical: Bell palsy (CN VII) affects all 2nd arch muscles → facial droop, hyperacusis (stapedius paralysis), loss of taste anterior 2/3 tongue.
Cricothyroid is a 4th arch muscle innervated by CN X (superior laryngeal branch).
Muscles of mastication are 1st arch derivatives innervated by CN V3, not CN VII.

explanation: Correct: Muscles of facial expression (2nd arch).

stem: The recurrent laryngeal nerve innervates muscles derived from which pharyngeal arch?

choices.feedback: Correct! 6th pharyngeal arch: Cartilage → cricoid, arytenoid, corniculate, cuneiform cartilages. Muscles: all intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT cricothyroid. Nerve: CN X — recurrent laryngeal branch. The left recurrent laryngeal nerve loops under the aortic arch (6th arch artery on left → ductus arteriosus/ligamentum arteriosum). The right recurrent laryngeal loops under the right subclavian artery. Damage → hoarseness (vocal cord paralysis). At-risk during thyroid surgery, cardiothoracic surgery, or from mediastinal tumors.
The 4th arch is innervated by the SUPERIOR laryngeal nerve (external branch of CN X), not the recurrent laryngeal.
3rd arch has one muscle (stylopharyngeus) innervated by CN IX.
2nd arch muscles (facial expression) are innervated by CN VII.

stem: What is the pathogenic sequence in Pierre Robin sequence?

choices.text: Neural crest deficiency → simultaneous mandibular and palatal hypoplasia
choices.feedback: While neural crest cells contribute to mandibular development, Pierre Robin is specifically a SEQUENCE where mandibular hypoplasia leads to the downstream events.

choices.text: Mandibular hypoplasia → tongue posteriorly displaced (glossoptosis) → U
choices.feedback: Correct! Pierre Robin SEQUENCE (not syndrome — it's a cascade of events): (1) Primary: mandibular hypoplasia (micrognathia) — the jaw fails to grow adequately in utero, (2) This positions the tongue posteriorly and superiorly (glossoptosis), (3) The tongue mechanically prevents the palatal shelves from fusing → U-shaped (not V-shaped) cleft palate, (4) After birth: glossoptosis causes upper airway obstruction (especially in supine position). Management: prone positioning, nasopharyngeal airway, mandibular distraction osteogenesis if severe. Can be isolated or part of Stickler syndrome (most common associated condition).
The causation runs the other direction. Mandibular hypoplasia is the primary event.

choices.text: Primary palatal defect → secondary mandibular undergrowth → tongue prolapse
choices.feedback: The cleft palate is SECONDARY to the tongue malposition, which is secondary to the micrognathia. The mandible is the primary defect.

explanation: Key detail: shaped cleft palate → airway obstruction.

stem: Pierre Robin sequence is most commonly associated with which underlying syndrome?

choices.feedback: Turner syndrome is associated with horseshoe kidney, coarctation of the aorta, and webbed neck, not Pierre Robin sequence.

Correct! ~50% of Pierre Robin cases are isolated, but when syndromic, Stickler syndrome is the most common association (~30%). Stickler syndrome: autosomal dominant collagen disorder (COL2A1, COL11A1) → Pierre Robin sequence + severe myopia/retinal detachment + joint hypermobility + sensorineural hearing loss + vitreous anomalies. All Pierre Robin patients should have ophthalmologic evaluation (early detection of retinal detachment is critical). Other associations: velocardiofacial syndrome (22q11.2), Treacher Collins, fetal alcohol syndrome.
Marfan syndrome involves fibrillin-1 (tall stature, aortic root dilation, lens subluxation) and is not associated with Pierre Robin.
Down syndrome has its own distinct craniofacial features and is not typically associated with Pierre Robin sequence.

stem: Treacher Collins syndrome results from deficiency of which gene and affects which embryologic structure?

choices.text: PAX3 gene affecting neural tube closure
choices.feedback: PAX3 mutations cause Waardenburg syndrome (pigmentary/hearing anomalies), not Treacher Collins.
SHH mutations cause holoprosencephaly, not Treacher Collins.

choices.text: TCOF1 gene affecting neural crest cell development of the 1st
choices.feedback: Correct! Treacher Collins syndrome (mandibulofacial dysostosis): autosomal dominant, TCOF1 gene mutation (90% of cases) → deficient neural crest cell proliferation and migration to the 1st and 2nd pharyngeal arches → bilateral, symmetric craniofacial hypoplasia. Features: malar (zygomatic) hypoplasia, mandibular hypoplasia, downslanting palpebral fissures, coloboma of lower eyelids, microtia/conductive hearing loss, choanal atresia. Unlike hemifacial microsomia (unilateral), Treacher Collins is always BILATERAL and symmetric.
FGFR2 mutations cause craniosynostosis syndromes (Crouzon, Apert), not Treacher Collins.

explanation: Best answer: TCOF1 gene affecting neural crest cell development of the 1st and 2nd pharyngeal arches.

stem: What distinguishes Treacher Collins syndrome from hemifacial microsomia clinically?

choices.text: Hemifacial microsomia is autosomal dominant; Treacher Collins is sporadic
choices.feedback: It's the opposite: Treacher Collins is autosomal dominant (TCOF1), while hemifacial microsomia is usually sporadic.

choices.text: Treacher Collins involves the ear; hemifacial microsomia does not
choices.feedback: Both conditions can involve ear anomalies (microtia). The key distinction is laterality.

choices.text: Treacher Collins has intellectual disability; hemifacial microsomia does not
choices.feedback: Intelligence is typically NORMAL in Treacher Collins syndrome. Cognitive impairment is not a distinguishing feature.

choices.text: Treacher Collins is bilateral and symmetric; hemifacial microsomia is typically unilateral
choices.feedback: Correct! Key distinguishing feature: Treacher Collins → BILATERAL, symmetric craniofacial hypoplasia affecting both sides equally. Hemifacial microsomia → predominantly UNILATERAL (70% right-sided), asymmetric. Both affect 1st/2nd arch derivatives. Treacher Collins: genetic (TCOF1), predictable bilateral pattern, autosomal dominant (50% recurrence). Hemifacial microsomia: usually sporadic, variable severity, part of OAVS/Goldenhar spectrum if vertebral and epibulbar dermoid features present.

stem: During cardiac development, the heart tube undergoes rightward looping (D-looping). Failure of this process results in which condition?
explanation: The primitive heart tube loops to the right (D-loop) during week 4, positioning the ventricles correctly. Failure → L-looping → dextrocardia (heart on right side). If accompanied by reversed organ asymmetry = situs inversus totalis (often benign). Isolated dextrocardia without situs inversus has high association with other cardiac defects. Kartagener syndrome = situs inversus + bronchiectasis + sinusitis (ciliary dyskinesia). [StatPearls NBK459312]

feedback.correct: Correct! Failed D-looping → L-looping → dextrocardia. Kartagener syndrome includes situs inversus.
feedback.incorrect: Cardiac looping failure → dextrocardia. Situs inversus totalis is usually benign; isolated dextrocardia is not.

stem: The foramen ovale allows blood to bypass which structure during fetal circulation?
explanation: Fetal circulation bypasses the lungs (high pulmonary vascular resistance) via three shunts: (1) Foramen ovale — right-to-left atrial shunt, (2) Ductus arteriosus — PA to aorta shunt, (3) Ductus venosus — umbilical vein to IVC (bypasses liver). At birth, increased PO2 and decreased prostaglandins close the DA (becomes ligamentum arteriosum). Increased left atrial pressure closes the foramen ovale (becomes fossa ovalis). [StatPearls NBK459312]

feedback.correct: Correct! Foramen ovale bypasses lungs (R→L atrial shunt). Three fetal shunts: FO, DA, DV.
feedback.incorrect: Fetal lung bypass: foramen ovale (atrial) + ductus arteriosus (PA→aorta). Close at birth with O2 exposure.

stem: Neural crest cells contribute to which cardiac structure, and what defects result from their failure to migrate?

choices.text: Aorticopulmonary septum; conotruncal defects (TOF, TGA, truncus arteriosus)

explanation: Neural crest cells migrate to form the aorticopulmonary (AP) septum, which divides the truncus arteriosus into the aorta and pulmonary artery. Failed neural crest migration → conotruncal defects: persistent truncus arteriosus, transposition of great arteries, tetralogy of Fallot, DORV. DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion) involves neural crest deficiency → cardiac defects + absent thymus/parathyroids + facial abnormalities. [StatPearls NBK459312]

feedback.correct: Correct! Neural crest → AP septum. Failure → conotruncal defects. DiGeorge = neural crest deficiency.
feedback.incorrect: Neural crest cells divide the truncus arteriosus. Failure → TOF, TGA, truncus arteriosus. DiGeorge (22q11.2).

stem: The neural plate folds to form the neural tube. Failure of anterior neuropore closure results in which defect?
explanation: Neural tube closure occurs during weeks 3-4: anterior neuropore closes by day 25, posterior neuropore by day 28. Failed anterior closure → anencephaly (absence of forebrain/calvarium, incompatible with life). Failed posterior closure → spina bifida spectrum (occulta, meningocele, myelomeningocele). Folic acid supplementation (400 mcg daily) before conception reduces NTD risk by 50-70%. [StatPearls NBK519519]

feedback.correct: Correct! Anterior neuropore failure → anencephaly. Posterior → spina bifida. Folic acid prevents both.
feedback.incorrect: Anterior = anencephaly (fatal). Posterior = spina bifida. Folic acid before conception reduces risk 50-70%.

stem: From which embryonic layer does the central nervous system develop?
explanation: The CNS develops from neuroectoderm. The notochord induces the overlying ectoderm to thicken into the neural plate (neurulation). The neural plate folds → neural groove → neural tube (CNS: brain + spinal cord). Neural crest cells (at the junction of neural plate and surface ectoderm) form the PNS, melanocytes, craniofacial bones, and adrenal medulla. Sonic hedgehog (SHH) from the notochord patterns the ventral neural tube. [StatPearls NBK519519]

feedback.correct: Correct! CNS = neuroectoderm (neural tube). PNS = neural crest. Notochord induces neurulation.
feedback.incorrect: Ectoderm → neuroectoderm → neural plate → neural tube (CNS). Neural crest → PNS + many other structures.

stem: Which signaling molecule from the notochord induces ventral patterning of the neural tube (including motor neuron specification)?
explanation: Sonic hedgehog (SHH) is secreted by the notochord and floor plate, establishing ventral neural tube identity (motor neurons, ventral interneurons). BMPs from the roof plate establish dorsal identity (sensory neurons). The concentration gradient of SHH vs BMP patterns the entire dorsoventral axis. Holoprosencephaly results from SHH signaling defects (failure of forebrain to divide into hemispheres). [StatPearls NBK519519]

feedback.correct: Correct! SHH from notochord = ventral (motor). BMP from roof plate = dorsal (sensory). SHH defect → holoprosencephaly.
feedback.incorrect: SHH patterns ventral neural tube (motor neurons). BMP patterns dorsal (sensory). SHH failure → holoprosencephaly.

stem: The midgut herniates through the umbilical ring during weeks 6-10 and rotates 270° counterclockwise. What is the consequence of incomplete rotation?

choices.text: Intestinal malrotation with risk of midgut volvulus

explanation: Normal midgut rotation: 270° CCW around the superior mesenteric artery. Incomplete rotation → malrotation → narrow mesenteric base → risk of midgut volvulus (life-threatening twisting). Presents with bilious vomiting in a neonate. Upper GI series shows abnormal position of duodenojejunal junction (ligament of Treitz). Ladd procedure is the surgical treatment. [StatPearls NBK560750]

feedback.correct: Correct! Incomplete 270° rotation → malrotation → volvulus risk. Bilious vomiting in neonate = emergency.
feedback.incorrect: Midgut rotates 270° CCW. Failure → malrotation → narrow base → volvulus. Ladd procedure corrects it.

stem: Which portion of the GI tract is supplied by the superior mesenteric artery and represents the embryologic midgut?

choices.text: Distal duodenum to proximal two-thirds of transverse colon

explanation: Embryologic divisions: Foregut (celiac trunk) = esophagus to proximal duodenum + liver, gallbladder, pancreas, spleen. Midgut (SMA) = distal duodenum to proximal 2/3 transverse colon. Hindgut (IMA) = distal 1/3 transverse colon to upper rectum. These vascular territories persist into adult anatomy and determine patterns of ischemia. [StatPearls NBK560750]

feedback.correct: Correct! Midgut (SMA) = distal duodenum to proximal 2/3 transverse colon.
feedback.incorrect: Foregut = celiac, Midgut = SMA, Hindgut = IMA. The embryologic divisions define adult vascular anatomy.

stem: Failure of the vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct to obliterate results in which congenital anomaly?
explanation: Meckel diverticulum is the most common congenital GI anomaly (~2% of population). Rule of 2s: 2% prevalence, 2 feet from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric/pancreatic), presents before age 2. Results from incomplete obliteration of the vitelline duct connecting the midgut to the yolk sac. Presents with painless lower GI bleeding (ectopic gastric mucosa → ulceration). Technetium-99m pertechnetate scan detects ectopic gastric tissue. [StatPearls NBK560750]

feedback.correct: Correct! Vitelline duct remnant → Meckel diverticulum (rule of 2s). Painless GI bleed in young child.
feedback.incorrect: Vitelline duct persistence → Meckel diverticulum. Rule of 2s. Tc-99m scan detects ectopic gastric mucosa.

stem: During which stage of lung development does surfactant production begin?
explanation: Lung development stages: Embryonic (3-7 wks, lung bud), Pseudoglandular (5-17 wks, conducting airways), Canalicular (16-25 wks, terminal bronchioles + type II pneumocytes begin), Saccular (24-36 wks, primitive alveoli), Alveolar (36 wks-8 yrs, mature alveoli). Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant starting in the canalicular stage, with adequate levels by ~35 weeks. Premature infants <35 weeks → RDS from surfactant deficiency. [StatPearls NBK544371]

feedback.correct: Correct! Surfactant begins in the canalicular stage. Adequate production by ~35 weeks.
feedback.incorrect: Type II pneumocytes appear in the canalicular stage. Surfactant adequate at ~35 weeks. Before that → RDS risk.

stem: Antenatal betamethasone is given to accelerate fetal lung maturity. What does it stimulate?
explanation: Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 12 mg IM × 2 doses, 24 hours apart) stimulate type II pneumocytes to produce surfactant, reducing RDS incidence and neonatal mortality. Most effective when given 24-48 hours before delivery. Indicated for threatened preterm birth at 24-34 weeks (some guidelines extend to 36+6). Also reduces risk of IVH and NEC. One of the most evidence-based interventions in obstetrics. [StatPearls NBK544371]

feedback.correct: Correct! Betamethasone stimulates surfactant production by type II pneumocytes. Give at 24-34 weeks.
feedback.incorrect: Antenatal steroids = surfactant induction → reduces RDS, IVH, NEC. Most effective 24-48 hours before delivery.

stem: The trachea and esophagus develop from the same embryonic structure. Which one?
explanation: Both the trachea and esophagus develop from the foregut. The tracheoesophageal septum divides the ventral respiratory bud from the dorsal esophagus during week 4. Incomplete separation → tracheoesophageal fistula (TEF). The most common type (85%): proximal esophageal atresia with distal TEF. Polyhydramnios occurs because the fetus cannot swallow amniotic fluid effectively. [StatPearls NBK544371]

feedback.correct: Correct! Foregut → trachea (ventral) + esophagus (dorsal). TE septum divides them.
feedback.incorrect: Trachea and esophagus both from foregut. Failed septation → TEF. Proximal EA + distal TEF is 85% of cases.

stem: The metanephros develops into the permanent kidney. What structure induces nephron formation by invading the metanephric mesoderm?
explanation: The ureteric bud (from the mesonephric/Wolffian duct) invades the metanephric mesenchyme, inducing nephron formation through reciprocal signaling. The ureteric bud forms the collecting system (collecting ducts, calyces, renal pelvis, ureter). The metanephric mesenchyme forms the nephrons (glomerulus through DCT). Failure → renal agenesis. Duplicated ureteric bud → duplex collecting system. [StatPearls NBK557515]

feedback.correct: Correct! Ureteric bud → collecting system. Metanephric mesenchyme → nephrons. Reciprocal induction.
feedback.incorrect: Ureteric bud invades metanephric mesoderm. Bud = collecting system, mesenchyme = nephrons.

stem: Three sequential kidney systems develop in the embryo. Which is the definitive adult kidney?
explanation: Three kidneys develop sequentially: Pronephros (weeks 3-4, degenerates — non-functional), Mesonephros (weeks 4-8, functions temporarily, forms mesonephric/Wolffian duct), Metanephros (week 5 onwards, becomes permanent kidney). The Wolffian duct persists as male reproductive structures (epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles). Müllerian (paramesonephric) duct forms female structures. [StatPearls NBK557515]

feedback.correct: Correct! Metanephros = definitive kidney. Mesonephros → Wolffian duct (male repro). Pronephros degenerates.
feedback.incorrect: Pro → Meso → Meta. Metanephros is the adult kidney starting week 5. Wolffian duct persists in males.

stem: Limb buds appear during which week of embryonic development, and what signaling center controls proximal-distal growth?
explanation: Limb buds appear week 4 (upper slightly before lower). The apical ectodermal ridge (AER) at the tip drives proximal-to-distal outgrowth via FGF signaling. Zone of polarizing activity (ZPA) in the posterior mesenchyme controls anterior-posterior patterning via SHH (determines digit identity). AER removal → limb truncation. Thalidomide disrupted limb outgrowth → phocomelia. [StatPearls NBK554393]

feedback.correct: Correct! AER (FGFs) = proximal-distal growth. ZPA (SHH) = anterior-posterior patterning. Week 4 onset.
feedback.incorrect: Limbs appear week 4. AER drives outgrowth (proximal→distal). ZPA determines which digit is which.

stem: The thyroid gland develops from the foramen cecum of the tongue and descends to its adult position. What remnant marks the path of descent?
explanation: The thyroid develops from endodermal tissue at the foramen cecum (base of tongue) during week 3-4 and descends along the thyroglossal duct to its adult pre-tracheal position by week 7. The duct normally obliterates. Persistence → thyroglossal duct cyst (midline neck mass that elevates with swallowing and tongue protrusion). Ectopic thyroid tissue can occur anywhere along the descent path (lingual thyroid most common). [StatPearls NBK557595]

feedback.correct: Correct! Thyroglossal duct = descent path. Cyst elevates with swallowing/tongue protrusion. Midline.
feedback.incorrect: Thyroid descends from foramen cecum via thyroglossal duct. Cyst = midline mass that moves with swallowing.

stem: The mesonephric (Wolffian) duct develops into male reproductive structures under the influence of which hormone?
explanation: Testosterone from fetal Leydig cells stabilizes the mesonephric (Wolffian) duct, which differentiates into the epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles, and ejaculatory ducts. In females, absence of testosterone causes Wolffian duct regression. Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) from Sertoli cells causes regression of the paramesonephric (Müllerian) duct in males. [StatPearls NBK557819]

feedback.correct: Correct! Testosterone → Wolffian duct → male internal structures (epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles).
feedback.incorrect: Testosterone stabilizes Wolffian duct. AMH regresses Müllerian duct. Both needed for male differentiation.

stem: The paramesonephric (Müllerian) duct gives rise to which female structures?

choices.text: Fallopian tubes, uterus, and upper two-thirds of the vagina

explanation: The Müllerian duct forms the fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix, and upper two-thirds of the vagina. In males, AMH from Sertoli cells causes Müllerian regression. Failure of Müllerian duct fusion → bicornuate or septate uterus. Complete Müllerian agenesis (Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser syndrome) → absent uterus/upper vagina with normal ovaries and 46,XX karyotype. [StatPearls NBK557819]

feedback.correct: Correct! Müllerian duct → fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix, upper 2/3 vagina. Fusion defects → uterine anomalies.
feedback.incorrect: Müllerian duct = female internal organs. Ovaries arise separately from gonadal ridge, not from Müllerian duct.

stem: Which of the three embryonic kidney systems persists as the definitive adult kidney?
explanation: Three sequential kidney systems develop: pronephros (nonfunctional, degenerates by week 4), mesonephros (functions briefly, contributes to male reproductive ducts), and metanephros (appears week 5, becomes the permanent kidney). The metanephros develops from two sources: the ureteric bud (from mesonephric duct → collecting system, ureter, pelvis, calyces) and the metanephric mesoderm (→ nephrons, Bowman capsule, tubules). [StatPearls NBK557819]

stem: A newborn has bilateral flank masses and severe oligohydramnios. Ultrasound shows multicystic dysplastic kidneys. What is the embryologic mechanism?

choices.text: Failure of ureteric bud to interact with metanephric mesoderm

explanation: Multicystic dysplastic kidney results from failure of the ureteric bud to properly interact with the metanephric mesoderm, leading to abnormal nephron induction. Bilateral cases cause Potter sequence (oligohydramnios → pulmonary hypoplasia, limb deformities, flattened facies). Other renal anomalies from abnormal ureteric bud development: duplex collecting system, renal agenesis, vesicoureteral reflux. [StatPearls NBK557819]

stem: Limb development is patterned along three axes. Which signaling center controls the proximal-distal axis?
explanation: The AER (apical ectodermal ridge) secretes FGFs that maintain the underlying mesenchyme in a proliferative state, driving proximal-to-distal limb outgrowth. The ZPA (posterior mesoderm, secretes Sonic hedgehog/SHH) patterns the anterior-posterior (thumb-to-pinky) axis. Wnt-7a from dorsal ectoderm establishes dorsal-ventral patterning. Loss of AER → limb truncation (amelia/meromelia). [StatPearls NBK554393]

stem: Somites differentiate into which three key derivatives?
explanation: Each somite differentiates into: sclerotome (ventromedial → vertebrae and ribs), myotome (central → skeletal muscle), and dermatome (dorsolateral → dermis of the back). Somites form in a cranial-to-caudal sequence beginning around day 20. There are ~42-44 somite pairs. The notochord induces sclerotome differentiation via SHH signaling. [StatPearls NBK554393]

feedback.incorrect: Somite derivatives: sclerotome (bone), myotome (muscle), dermatome (skin). Notochord/SHH drives sclerotome.

stem: The adrenal cortex and adrenal medulla have different embryologic origins. Which germ layer gives rise to each?

choices.text: Cortex from mesoderm; medulla from neural crest
Cortex from endoderm; medulla from ectoderm

explanation: The adrenal cortex develops from mesoderm (intermediate mesoderm/urogenital ridge), producing corticosteroids. The adrenal medulla develops from neural crest cells (same origin as sympathetic ganglia), producing catecholamines. This dual origin explains why pheochromocytoma (medullary tumor) follows the 'rule of 10s' and is associated with MEN 2 syndromes (neural crest origin). [StatPearls NBK557717]

feedback.incorrect: Dual origin: cortex from mesoderm, medulla from neural crest. Explains different tumors and hormones.

stem: In males, the mesonephric (Wolffian) duct develops into the epididymis, vas deferens, and seminal vesicles under the influence of which hormone?
explanation: Sexual differentiation: SRY gene on Y chromosome → Sertoli cells produce AMH (regresses Müllerian ducts) and Leydig cells produce testosterone (maintains Wolffian ducts → epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles, ejaculatory ducts). DHT (from testosterone via 5α-reductase) develops external genitalia (penis, scrotum, prostate). Without testosterone, Wolffian ducts degenerate; without AMH, Müllerian ducts persist → uterus, fallopian tubes, upper vagina. [StatPearls NBK557515]

feedback.incorrect: Testosterone (not DHT) drives Wolffian duct development. DHT handles external genitalia. AMH eliminates Müllerian ducts.

stem: A newborn male has bilateral cryptorchidism and hypospadias. Karyotype reveals 46,XY. Which enzyme deficiency could explain failure of external genital masculinization?
explanation: 5α-reductase converts testosterone to DHT, which is required for development of the prostate, penis, and scrotum and for testicular descent. Deficiency → ambiguous genitalia at birth (appears female or undervirilized male) with Wolffian structures intact (testosterone is normal). stem: Bilateral renal agenesis results in oligohydramnios and a characteristic set of findings known as what?
explanation: Potter sequence: bilateral renal agenesis → no fetal urine → severe oligohydramnios → fetal compression → limb deformities, flat facies (Potter facies), pulmonary hypoplasia (lethal). The lungs cannot develop without adequate amniotic fluid for fetal breathing movements. Unilateral renal agenesis is compatible with life (compensatory hypertrophy of the remaining kidney). Any cause of severe oligohydramnios can cause Potter-like features. [StatPearls NBK557515]

feedback.incorrect: Potter sequence: no kidneys → no urine → no amniotic fluid → compressed fetus + underdeveloped lungs. Fatal.

stem: During kidney ascent from the pelvis to the lumbar region, the kidneys may fuse at their lower poles. What is this anomaly called, and at what structure does ascent become arrested?

choices.text: Duplex kidney; arrested at renal hilum
Horseshoe kidney; arrested at the inferior mesenteric artery
Pelvic kidney; arrested at the sacral promontory
Crossed fused ectopia; arrested at the aortic bifurcation

explanation: Horseshoe kidney: fusion of the lower poles during ascent, forming an isthmus that gets trapped under the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), preventing further ascent. Present in ~1/400-600 births. Usually asymptomatic but increased risk of UPJ obstruction, stones, and infections due to altered drainage. Associated with Turner syndrome (45,X). Most horseshoe kidneys function normally. [StatPearls NBK557515]

feedback.incorrect: Fused lower poles get caught under the IMA → horseshoe kidney. Turner syndrome association. Usually functional.

stem: Somites differentiate into three components. Which somite derivative forms the vertebral column and ribs?

Vertebral body formation involves sclerotome resegmentation (cranial half of one somite fuses with caudal half of the next). [StatPearls NBK554393]

stem: The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis) develops from which embryonic structure?
explanation: The anterior pituitary develops from Rathke pouch, an upward evagination of oral ectoderm (stomodeum).
